coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from Actinidia deliciosa and their catalytic activity and cytotoxicity applications
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, Velusamy
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using Actinidia deliciosa (A. deliciosa) fruit extract as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen dopant is reported here. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The average size of the N-CDs was approximately 3.59 nm and the calculated inter layer distance was found to be 0.21 nm. Raman spectroscopy and SAED pattern revealed the graphitic nature of the synthesized N-CDs. The N-CDs were found to emit intense blue color at 405 nm under the excitation of 315 nm. The doping of nitrogen over the surface of the N-CDs was confirmed by EDS, FT-IR and XPS studies. The synthesized N-CDs were found to exhibit excellent catalytic activity in the reduction of Rhodamine-B using sodium borohydrate. The MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs towards L-929 and MCF-7 cells. From the results obtained, it was found that the N-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility on both L-929 and MCF-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Actinidia deliciosa
Fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots
Rhodamine-B
Catalytic activity
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of A. deliciosa fruit extract
Optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs
Catalytic activity of N-CDs in the reduction of Rhodamine-B using NaBH4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of the prepared N-CDs at 20 nm scale; (b) High resolution image of prepared N-CDs at 2 nm scale; (c) Size distribution chart for N-CDs; and (d) SAED pattern of N-CDs.
EDS spectrum of the prepared N-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of A. deliciosa extract and synthesized N-CDs; (b) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of synthesized N-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 265 nm to 315 nm; (b) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths from 315 nm to 380 nm.
FT-IR spectra of A. deliciosa extract and prepared N-CDs.
XRD pattern of the synthesized N-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan survey spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs; (b) high resolution XP spectrum of C1s; (c) high resolution XP spectrum of N1 s; and (d) high resolution XP spectrum of O1s.
UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of RhB using NaBH4 (a) in the absence of N-CDs; (b)in the presence of N-CDs.
Microscopic images of L-929 cells after incubation of 24 h with various concentrations of N-CDs (a) 0 μL/mL (b) 5 μL/mL (c) 10 μL/mL (d) 15 μL/mL (e) 20 μL/mL and (f) 25 μL/mL using MTT assay.
Microscopic images of MCF-7 cells after incubation of 24 h with various concentrations of N-CDs (a) 0 μL/mL (b) 5 μL/mL (c) 10 μL/mL (d) 15 μL/mL (e) 20 μL/mL and (f) 25 μL/mL using MTT assay.
Cell viability (%) of L-929 cells after incubation of 24 h with various concentrations of synthesized N-CDs.
Cell viability (%) of MCF-7 cells after incubation of 24 h with various concentrations of synthesized N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Facile green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from Actinidia deliciosa and their catalytic activity and cytotoxicity applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) using Actinidia deliciosa (A. deliciosa) fruit extract as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen dopant is reported here. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The average size of the N-CDs was approximately 3.59 nm and the calculated inter layer distance was found to be 0.21 nm. Raman spectroscopy and SAED pattern revealed the graphitic nature of the synthesized N-CDs. The N-CDs were found to emit intense blue color at 405 nm under the excitation of 315 nm. The doping of nitrogen over the surface of the N-CDs was confirmed by EDS, FT-IR and XPS studies. The synthesized N-CDs were found to exhibit excellent catalytic activity in the reduction of Rhodamine-B using sodium borohydrate. The MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of N-CDs towards L-929 and MCF-7 cells. From the results obtained, it was found that the N-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility on both L-929 and MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The new class of carbon materials viz., “carbon dots” (CDs) with a size less than 10 nm in diameter have attracted the attention of Scientists in recent years due to their distinctive properties compared to inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) [1]. The unique properties of CDs such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high water solubility, excellent optical properties, catalytic properties, electrical conductivity, high stability and easy functionalization are responsible for their utility in applications such as catalysis, bio-imaging, cellular imaging, metal sensing, drug delivery, and energy storage [2–6]. Size, nature of dopants, reaction time, reaction temperature, pH and solvents play a major role in enhancing properties and also for the various applications of CDs [7,8]. To improve photoluminescent properties for better catalytic, biological and sensing applications, heteroatoms such as B, N, S, and P are added as dopants during the synthesis of CDs [9,10]. Among these, nitrogen doped CDs are used as catalysts and fluorescent probes in recent years due to their enhanced properties.

Now a days, economical and eco-friendly green chemistry concepts attract researchers for the synthesis of CDs [11]. Inexpensive and renewable sources like fruit juices and green wastes such as watermelon peel, pineapple peel, cucumber peel etc., have been used as a starting materials for the preparation of CDs [12,13]. For the synthesis of CDs, most commonly used methods are chemical oxidation, ultrasonic synthesis, hydrothermal-carbonization, solvothermal synthesis, microwave synthesis, and laser ablation. Among these methods, hydrothermal-carbonization method is a facile method, because it is eco-friendly, simple, affordable, a soft chemical route which results in uniform particle size for the synthesis of CDs, which serve as excellent candidates for the catalytic and bioimaging applications [14–18].

Actinidia deliciosa (A. deliciosa) belonging to the Actinidiaceae family, is commonly known as “kiwi fruit”. Presently kiwi fruits are commercially cultivated in many countries including the United States, Italy, Chile, France, Greece, Brazil, and Japan as well as in India and Sri Lanka [19] and it is sold worldwide due to its medicinal values to treat diseases such as hepatitis, oedema, rheumatoid arthritis, gastric cancer and breast cancer [20]. The major phytochemicals available in the crude extracts of pulp are σ-tocomonoenol, α-tocopherol, σ-tocopherol, caffeic acid glucosyl derivatives, β-sitosterol, stigmasterol chlorogenic acid, campesterol, kaempferol and quercetin [21,22]. The A. deliciosa fruits are rich in vitamin C and also contain vitamins A and E. In view of the above merits, the A. deliciosa fruits have been chosen as a carbon source for the preparation of CDs.

Rhodamine-B (RhB) is a carcinogenic xanthene based dye and it is commonly used in paint, textiles, paper and leather industries and also used as an illegal food additive [23,24]. It creates severe health problems such as skin and eye irritations, reproductive and developmental toxicity, neurotoxicity, chronic toxicity and cancer in both humans and animals [25,26]. Rhodamine-B is widely used in the biological applications, as a staining fluorescent dye and also a tracer dye [27,28]. Removal of RhB from textile effluents is a challenging task and there have been a number of attempts to remove RhB from effluents using different methods [29]. Most of the methods are based on photocatalysis. Methods involving photocatalytic degradation under UV/solar light using Cu2O/graphene oxide nanocomposite, titanosilicate/bismuth vanadate nanocomposites, nanostructured zinc doped cobalt ferrite and ZnO nanopowder etc., have been reported [30–33]. But most of the reported methods suffer from inherent difficulties when implemented at the larger scale. Hence, there is a need to develop methods which are facile, simple and at the same time efficient. Towards this objective, for the first time the N-CDs have been used as a catalyst in the degradation of RhB using NaBH4 in this work.

The present study was undertaken to synthesize nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) by simple hydrothermal-carbonization method using Actinidia deliciosa (A. deliciosa) fruit extract as a carbon precursor and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen source. The synthesized N-CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy techniques. UV–Visible (UV–Vis), fluorescence spectroscopy techniques were used for examining the optical properties of synthesized N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs were then used as a catalyst for the reduction of rhodamine-B dye by sodium borohydrate. The cytotoxicity of prepared N-CDs was evaluated on the L-929 (Lymphoblastoid-929) and MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) cells.

The A. deliciosa (Kiwi) fruits were procured from the local fruit shop in Dindigul, Tamil Nadu, India. The rhodamine - B (RhB) dye, aqueous ammonia, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT) (98%) and sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The L-929 and MCF-7 cells were procured from the National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune, India. Deionised (DI) water was used throughout the study for the solution preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of A. deliciosa fruit extract
ce:section.ce:para: The peel of A. deliciosa fruit was removed and the green inner flesh part containing black seeds in the middle was sliced into small pieces. An approximate amount (50–60 g) of sliced pieces of fruit was ground well using a mixer grinder. The well ground extract was first filtered by cotton to remove large particles and then filtered using Whatmann 40 filter paper to get clear extract. The pH of the clear extract was measured using pH meter and used for the preparation of N-CDs.

About 30 ml of A. deliciosa extract was taken into 50 ml Teflon lined autoclave and 1 ml of aq.NH3 solution (25%) was added. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a hot air oven. The addition of aq.NH3 was done for improving fluorescence behavior of the CDs. The resultant dark brown solution (N-CDs) was filtered using Whatmann 40 filter paper and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles to get a transparent solution of N-CDs. N-CDs obtained were kept in 10 °C for characterization and application studies.

The N-CDs prepared were subjected to HR-TEM analysis for which the sample was prepared by depositing a droplet of the synthesized N-CDs on carbon coated Cu grid which was allowed to dry at ambient temperature. After drying, the drop of N-CDs coated Cu grid, the images were obtained by using JEOL JEM 2100 Microscope (at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV). Elemental analysis (EDS) and SAED pattern were also performed using the same instrument.

UV–Visible spectra of N-CDs were recorded on a Perkin Elmer lambda 35 spectrometer while fluorescence spectra were obtained by using Jasco FP 8500 spectrophotometer at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was varied from lower to higher wavelengths (280–360 nm) to find the maximum luminescence intensity in fluorescence measurements with the 5 nm slit width and a scan rate of 240 nm/min.

Functional groups present in the N-CDs were obtained by FT-IR analysis using Jasco FT-IR 460 Plus spectrophotometer. The amorphous nature of the N-CDs was measured by a PANalytical XPERT X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) with Cu-Kα irradiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA). Raman spectra were recorded by using STR 500 nm focal length laser Raman spectroscopy.

The synthesized N-CDs were evaluated for their ability to catalyze the reduction of rhodamine-B using UV–Visible spectrometer. The optimum concentration of reactants (rhodamine-B & NaBH4) and catalyst (N-CDs) were found out by performing several experiments by trial and error. In the first reaction, 0.20 ml of freshly prepared ice-cooled NaBH4 (0.50 M) was added to 1 ml of rhodamine-B (1 × 10−5 M) and 1.8 ml of water. The final volume of the reaction mixture was made up to 3 ml in the 3.5 ml cuvette. This reaction was monitored at different time intervals. The synthesized N-CDs (50 μl) were added to the above reaction mixture in the second reaction, and this reduction reaction was monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy at time intervals of every 2 min.

The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was employed for the evaluation of cytotoxicity of synthesized N-CDs on L-929 and MCF-7 cells [34]. The Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) was used for the seeding of cells in a 96 well cultured plate with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) which was incubated for 24 h with 5% CO2 humidity at 37 °C. The incubated medium was removed and washed twice by phosphate buffer saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH 7.4) after 24 h incubation. Further, the synthesized N-CDs at different concentrations (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 μL/mL) were added to the cells and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in the absence of DMEM medium. After over 24 h of incubation, the cells were removed and washed again with PBS solution. The cell well plates were further incubated for 4 h at 37 °C with the addition of 10 μL of MTT solution (100 μg/mL) to each well. Afterwards, the 100 μL of 16% sodium dodecyl sulphate solution (solubilization solution) was used for dissolving the insoluble precipitate of MTT (formazan) in each well. The cell viability of L-929 and MCF-7 cells was measured by recording changes in absorbance at 570 nm using a plate reading spectrophotometer. The control experiments were carried out in the absence of N-CDs with similar conditions and procedure. The following formula was used to calculate the cell viability:
(1)
Cell
viability
( % )
= Treated
cells
OD / controlled
cells
OD × 100

The hydrothermal carbonization, which is a simple and eco-friendly method, was employed for the synthesis of N-CDs from A. deliciosa fruit extract. The green color of the fruit extract was changed to blackish brown, indicating the formation of carbon dots after 12 h of carbonization at 180 °C [35].

The surface morphology of synthesized N-CDs was obtained using HR-TEM. Fig. 1 shows HR-TEM images of prepared N-CDs in different scale ranges with SAED pattern and size distribution chart. As evident from Fig. 1a, the synthesized N-CDs were spherical in nature and evenly distributed over the carbon coated Cu grid. The synthesized N-CDs had well- ordered lattice fringes (Fig. 1b) with an interlayer distance of 0.21 nm which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (100) [36,37]. The average size of the synthesized N-CDs was approximately 3.59 nm with diameter ranging from 1.5 to 7 nm (Fig. 1c). Furthermore, the diffused ring pattern of SAED for N-CDs (Fig. 1d) suggested the amorphous phase of carbon [38].

The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) is the corresponding technique of HR-TEM and this technique clearly provides details on the elements present in the synthesized N-CDs. As evident from EDS spectrum of prepared N-CDs (Fig. 2 .), the prepared N-CDs mainly composed of C (74.59%), N (6.88%) and O (18.53%), which confirmed the doping of nitrogen and oxygen atom over the surface of the CDs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs were obtained using UV–Visible and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The excitation spectra of A. deliciosa extract and synthesized N-CDs are displayed in Fig. 3 a and b. The A. deliciosa extract showed absorption maxima (λmax) at 282 nm and the synthesized N-CDs revealed two distinct peaks at 277 nm and 315 nm (Fig. 3a).

They arise due to π-π* and n-π* transitions associated with C=C and carbonyl/hydroxyl groups, respectively [39,40]. Addition of aqueous ammonia with A. deliciosa extract during the hydrothermal reaction caused the conversion of phenolic –OH to ammonium phenolate ions resulting in changes of absorption values of N-CDs [41]. The excitation spectrum of synthesized N-CDs (Fig. 3b) had two prominent peaks at 289 nm and 315 nm, which could be correlated to the absorption spectrum of the prepared N-CDs (Fig. 3a).

The detailed fluorescence study on the N-CDs was carried out under various excitation wavelengths from 265 nm to 380 nm with the wavelength interval of 5 nm and then the maximum emission intensity was observed at 315 nm. The excitation dependant emission spectra are given in Fig. 4 . The fluorescent intensity was gradually increased and there was no notable shift during the increase of excitation wavelength from 265 nm to 315 nm and this could be due to the π-π* transitions of graphitic carbon cores (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the emission intensity decreased as the excitation wavelength increased from 315 nm to 380 nm and the emission peaks were red shifted (Fig. 4b).

The most common and attractive photo physical property of carbon dots (CDs) is the excitation dependent emission. This is ascribed to different sizes of CDs and distribution of different surface states because of the presence of various organic functional groups over the surface of the CDs [41,42]. Typically, in the case of small size CDs (<5 nm) emission maxima strongly depend on excitation wavelength. Due to this reason, the emission intensity was gradually increased while increasing excitation wavelength from 265 to 315 nm in fluorescent spectra. Since, the excitation was selectively made in the region of π-π* transition no notable shift was observed due to the absence of multi-fluorescence centers. After increasing excitation wavelength from 315 to 380 nm which is specific of n-π* transition region, the emission intensity got shifted to higher wavelength (i.e. red shift) which can be attributed to the presence of amine functionalities in N-CDs [44]. The radiative recombination (π* to n transitions) of carbonyl/amine functionalities in the surface is the major reason for arising excitation dependent emission spectra of N-CDs [45,46]. The maximal intensity (405 nm) of N-CDs was noticed at the excitation wavelength of 315 nm. Hence, this confirmed that the synthesized N-CDs are suitable candidates for bioimaging and biolabeling applications due to the wide ranges of fluorescent spectra [43].

The emission mechanism of N-CDs can be explained on the basis of proposed mechanism by Xu et al. [47]. Emission intensity of CDs was enhanced due to the doping of nitrogen through stimulating the upward shift of the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [48]. The hexagonal rings of carbon in the CDs were disturbed due to the formation of C ̶ N bond in the doping process and this tends to create the emission energy traps. The recombination of electron hole-pairs in the strongly localized π and π* electronic levels of sp2 sites trigger the surface defect derived fluorescence [12,49–51]. Infact, surface functionalization make surface defects more stable which facilitate the recombination of surface electrons and holes thereby leading to fluorescent emissions [52].
Image 2

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of A. deliciosa extract and prepared NCDs. The FT-IR spectrum of A. deliciosa extract exhibits absorption bands at 3422, 2928, 1732, 1199 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching frequencies of phenolic –OH, –C-H, –C=O and –C-O-C functional groups, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs show the absorption bands at 3413, 2926, 1704 and 1114 cm−1, which reveal the functional groups of A. deliciosa have been shifted slightly. In the IR spectrum of the prepared N-CDs, –O-H stretching was shifted from 3422 to 3413 cm−1, which reveals that the doping of nitrogen by amine functionalities and the stretching vibration of –C-H groups had shifted from 2928 to 2926 cm−1 [41]. The broad absorption band of prepared N-CDs appearing at 1644 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic vibration of the C=C (sp2) stretching mode [53]. The weak absorption band at 1049 cm−1 appears from the stretching of –C-N groups in N-CDs [54]. Due to the influence of carbonyl groups, the functional group intensities of the A. deliciosa extract and N-CDs (–C=O and –C-O-C) were decreased, shifted and broadened. Hence, the presence of acid, amine, hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties over the surface of synthesized N-CDs could be confirmed from the FT-IR spectrum. Moreover, the phytoconstituents of A. deliciosa are present over the surface of the synthesized N-CDs, which are responsible for the water solubility and the optoelectronic and biological applications.

Fig. 6 presents the XRD pattern of synthesized N-CDs and this displays a very intense broad peak around 2θ = 28.5° and a weak peak at 2θ = 40.3° which correspond to (002) and (001) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon [36,55]. From the Bragg's equation (Eq. (2)) the d-spacing value for synthesized N-CDs were calculated.
(2)
nλ = 2 d
Sinθ
( or )
d = nλ / 2
Sinθ
where, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-rays (λ = 1.54 Å) and θ is the angle of plane. The calculated d-spacing value of N-CDs was found to be approximately 0.322 nm and 0.216 nm for C (002) and C (100) planes, respectively.

Raman spectroscopy is the useful technique for characterization of carbon related nanomaterials. Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectrum of synthesized N-CDs. The spectrum exhibits two distinct Raman bands at 1354 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1, which belong to the sp3 defects (D band) and sp2 carbon (G band) in the synthesized N-CDs [56]. The D band is related to vibrations of carbon atoms for distorted graphite and G band belongs to the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice sp2 carbon atoms [16]. Generally, the well graphitized carbon materials have the intensities ratio of D band and G band (I
D/
I
G) to be less than 1 and the calculated I
D/
I
G ratio for the prepared N-CDs was 0.87, which denotes less defective carbon structures of the synthesized N-CDs [57]. Raman results confirmed the presence of minor surface defects graphitic structure in the synthesized N-CDs. XPS technique is widely used to characterize and clarify the chemical composition of the synthesized N-CDs [16].

Fig. 8 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs and high resolution XPS spectra of their three elements: C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s). The peak positions of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 8a) are at 285, 400 and 532 eV, respectively. The deconvolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 8b) revealed four peaks at 283.5, 284.4, 287.1 and 294.5 eV and they are attributed to C-H, C=C (sp2), C-N/O-C=O and π-π∗ transitions [1,16,58]. Especially, the C1s peak at 284.4 eV noticeably indicated that the synthesized N-CDs acquired mainly for sp2 carbons [59]. In the high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 8c) two binding energy peaks at 398.2 and 399.1 eV could be assigned to amine (C-NH2) and amide (O=C-NH2) groups, respectively, on the N-CDs surface [1,60]. The XP spectrum of O1s (Fig. 8d) was deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at 530.02, 531.2 and 532.3 eV corresponding to C=O, -C-O/-N-O and C-OH/C-O-C groups, respectively [1,16,41]. The XPS results clearly revealed that the synthesized N-CDs could have carbon, nitrogen and oxygen and their atomic percentage (%) was 74.6, 6.9 and 18.5%, which confirmed doping of nitrogen over the N-CDs surface along with oxygen [61]. The XPS results confirmed that the synthesized N-CDs were functionalized by hydroxyl, amine and carboxyl groups, which was further evidenced by HR-TEM with EDS analysis and FTIR spectroscopy studies [16,41].

The mechanism for the formation of N-CDs could involve polymerization, aromatization, nucleation and growth process [34]. In the first step, the inter and intra molecular dehydration of A. deliciosa extract takes place at above 100 °C which may lead to the formation of the bigger polymeric particles. In the next step, aromatization of polymeric particles will occur, which on increasing the temperature and in course time lead to the formation of aromatic clusters. These aromatic clusters may encounter the nucleation burst and lead to the formation of N-CDs [6,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of N-CDs in the reduction of Rhodamine-B using NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N-CDs were utilized as a catalyst in the reduction of a dye Rhodamine B (RhB) using NaBH4. The reaction was studied by observing the decrease in absorbance at 554 nm (characteristic peak of the dye) by UV–Visible spectroscopy. Fig. 9 (a) displays the UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of RhB using NaBH4 in the absence of N-CDs (catalyst) for a time period of 2 h. The intensity of RhB was decreased at minimum level after adding NaBH4 to RhB. However, no noticeable change could be observed while increasing the reaction time. Fig. 9 (b) shows the UV–Vis spectra of the reduction of RhB using NaBH4 in the presence of N-CDs. on the addition of 50 μL of N-CDs to the reaction cuvette containing RhB and NaBH4, the intensity of RhB is gradually decreased in regular time intervals of 2 min and the reaction was completed at 10 min, which clearly revealed that the synthesized N-CDs function as a catalyst in the reduction of RhB using NaBH4.

The Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism could be used to explain the degradation of RhB by the N-CDs. The RhB and the BH4 − ions from NaBH4 are absorbed on the N-CDs surface, respectively. After that, the electrons from BH4 ־ions have been transferred to RhB through N-CDs surface. Due to the electron transfer mediator nature of the N-CDs, the energy barrier between reactant and product were diminished [30,62,63]. Then, the rate of the reaction could be revamped and the reduction reaction was completed in 10 min. Furthermore, huge amount of H2 gas evolved during this reduction reaction. Hence, the large surface area and highly efficient electron-accepting potential of the prepared N-CDs is attributed for the excellent catalytic activity.

The results of the study indicate that by a simple one step carbonization, fluorescent N-CDs could be obtained from A. deliciosa extract using aqueous ammonia. The green synthesized N-CDs were found to emit blue fluorescence and had an average size of 3.59 nm with spherical shape. Further, the green synthesized N-CDs had graphitic nature with calculated interlayer distance of approximately 0.21 nm. When tested against L-929 and MCF-7 cells, the N-CDs were found to show a minimum level of anticancer activity. Further, it was found that N-CDs showed significant catalytic activity in the reduction of rhodamine-B in the presence of NaBH4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as fluorescent sensor for detection of explosive nitro compounds
Green chemistry approach for the synthesis of ZnO-carbon dots nanocomposites with good photocatalytic properties under visible light
Carbon dots prepared by solid state method via citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline for selective and sensing detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+

Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Microwave assisted green synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots: cytotoxicity and bio-imaging applications
Toward structurally defined carbon dots as ultracompact fluorescent probes
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe (III) detection
Green preparation of fluorescent carbon dots from lychee seeds and their application for the selective detection of methylene blue and imaging in living cells
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Synthesis of carbon dots from kitchen waste: conversion of waste to value added product
Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging using unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume
Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots originating from unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction
Synthesis, characterization and cells and tissues imaging of carbon quantum dots
Identification and assessment of antioxidant capacity of phytochemicals from kiwi fruits
Pigments in the fruit of red-fleshed kiwi fruit (Actinidia chinensis and Actinidia deliciosa)
A comparative study of microwave and chemically treated Acacia nilotica leaf as an eco friendly adsorbent for the removal of rhodamine B dye from aqueous solution
Development of a rapid, simple and sensitive HPLC-FLD method for determination of rhodamine B in chili-containing products
Removal of the hazardous dye rhodamine B through photocatalytic and adsorption treatments
Separation of toxic rhodamine B from aqueous solution using an efficient low-cost material, Azolla pinnata, by adsorption method
Fluorescent staining of elastic tissue with rhodamine B and related xanthene dyes
Dyes as tracers for vadose zone hydrology
Highly efficient catalytic reductive degradation of various organic dyes by Au/CeO2 -TiO2 nano-hybrid
Preparation of cubic Cu2O nanoparticles wrapped by reduced graphene oxide for the efficient removal of rhodamine B
Synthesis, characterization, and photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye under sunlight irradiation of porous titanosilicate (TS)/bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) nanocomposite hybrid catalyst
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye under UV/solar light using ZnO nanopowder synthesized by solution combustion route
Photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B under visible light using nanostructured zinc doped cobalt ferrite: kinetics and mechanism
